preFix.title: Understanding Part P building regulations
preFix.subtitle: What electrical work requires notification and what a homeowner can do
preFix.excerpt: An explanation of Part P of the Building Regulations: which types of electrical work in dwellings are notifiable, how to comply by using a registered electrician or notifying building control, and what work homeowners can carry out without notification.
preFix.sourceNotes: Building Regulations 2010 Part P. IET Wiring Regulations 18th Edition. Planning Portal Guidance on Electrical Work.

content.text: applies to electrical installations in dwellings in England and Wales. Its purpose is to reduce the risk of fire and electric shock from faulty wiring. Scotland and Northern Ireland have separate but similar regulations. The key question Part P asks is: is the work notifiable, and if so, who will certify it?

Notifiable work includes: adding a new circuit from the consumer unit; replacing the consumer unit; adding a circuit in a kitchen, bathroom, or garden; and fitting a socket, switch, or light in a bathroom. Any cable run in a kitchen or bathroom is notifiable. New circuits for electric vehicle chargers, solar PV, and heat pumps are also notifiable.
How to comply with notifiable work
There are two routes to compliance. The first: engage an electrician registered with a

such as NICEIC or NAPIT; they self-certify the work and notify building control on your behalf. The second: notify your local authority building control before starting; they will inspect and certify the work on completion. The first route is faster and usually cheaper.
What homeowners can do without notification

Minor work outside bathrooms and kitchens, and work that does not involve a new circuit, can be done by a competent homeowner without notification. This includes replacing sockets or switches like-for-like, replacing ceiling roses or pendant fittings, and adding a spur socket to an existing ring circuit. All work must still meet the IET Wiring Regulations.

Notifiable work done without certification causes problems when selling the property. Solicitors ask for Part P certificates for any electrical work done after 2005. Getting retrospective certification means an electrician must inspect the work and sign it off if it meets the Wiring Regulations. If the work does not comply, it must be fixed before the certificate can be issued.
